summary:
Sechín Bajo is an ancient site in the Casma Valley on the northern coast of Peru, with radiocarbon dates placing its earliest occupation at approximately 3500 BCE, making it one of the oldest known complex societies in the Americas. The site contains large platform mounds, circular sunken courts, and evidence of organized communal construction that predates the emergence of the better-known Norte Chico civilization by several centuries.

Archaeological investigations at Sechín Bajo, led by researchers from the Free University of Berlin in the 2000s and 2010s, revealed that the monumental architecture was built in successive stages over many generations, demonstrating sustained social organization and community investment in public construction. The site belongs to the broader phenomenon of early coastal Peruvian monument building that also includes [[event:1697|Aspero]] and the sites of [[event:37|Norte Chico]], suggesting that social complexity emerged along the Peruvian coast through maritime economies based on fishing and marine resource exploitation rather than agriculture.

read_more_links.title: Early Civilization in the Americas - Britannica
